explanation_md: `auto copy = limit` deduces `int`, not `const int` ŌĆö top-level `const` is stripped. So `copy = 20` is legal and modifies only `copy`. `limit` remains 10. Learners who expect a compile error or expect `copy` to remain 10 are applying the rule backwards: `const` on the source does not carry through `auto` deduction.

options.a: 99, because x is a reference to n
options.b: 5, because auto strips the reference and x is a copy
options.c: Undefined, because x and n alias the same memory
options.d: Compile error: cannot assign to auto-deduced variable

explanation_md: `auto x = ref` deduces `int` ŌĆö the reference is stripped ŌĆö so `x` is an independent copy of `n`. Modifying `x` does not affect `n`. To get an alias, you would need `auto& x = ref;`. Option a is the most common misconception: learners assume that because `ref` is a reference, anything copying from it must also be a reference.

`auto& r = a` deduces `int&` ŌĆö the `&` in the declaration forces `r` to be a reference to `a`. Writing `r = 7` modifies `a` directly through the reference. The output is 7. This is the correct way to use `auto` when you want an alias rather than a copy.

prompt_md: Given `const int* ptr` (a pointer to `const int`), what type does `auto p = ptr` deduce for `p`?

options.a: `int*` ŌĆö both the pointer and the const are stripped
options.b: `const int*` ŌĆö the low-level const on the pointee is preserved
options.c: `const int* const` ŌĆö both the pointer and the pointee are const
options.d: `int` ŌĆö auto deduces the value type by following the pointer

explanation_md: `auto` strips top-level `const` (const on the pointer variable itself) but keeps low-level `const` (const on what the pointer points to). Since `const int*` means the pointer points to a `const int`, the `const int` part is the pointee type ŌĆö it is low-level and is preserved. The deduced type is `const int*`. Option a is the common error of thinking all `const` is dropped.

options.b: `auto r = value` deduces `int`, not `int&`, so `r` is a copy and modifying it does not change `value`
options.c: `value` should be declared `const` for `auto` to deduce the reference
options.d: `auto` cannot be used with multiplication on the right-hand side

explanation_md: `auto r = value` gives `r` the type `int` ŌĆö a fresh copy ŌĆö because `auto` strips references. Assigning to `r` has no effect on `value`. The fix is `auto& r = value;` which forces `r` to be an `int&` alias for `value`.
